data.title: CAC 40 Moderately Lower At Noon
data.description:
BRUSSELS (dpa-AFX) - Despite easing geopolitical concerns, the French market traded weak Friday morning as investors made cautious moves, reacting to regional PMI readings.

The benchmark CAC 40 was down 41.04 or 0.52% at 8,107.85 a few minutes before noon.

Bouygues, Saint Gobain, ArcelorMittal, Stellantis, Societe Generale, Eiffage, Sanofi, Legrand, Michelin, Danone, LVMH and Hermes International lost 1 to 2%.

Kering, Vinci, Schneider Electric, Accor, Bureau Veritas, STMicroElectronics, Renault and EssilorLuxottica also traded weak.

Thales climbed 1.8%. Safran moved up by a little over 1%, and TotalEnergies lost nearly 1%. Credit Agricole and Veolia Environment posted modest gains.

Survey results from S&P Global showed the French private sector experienced a contraction in January, the first time in three months, as clients were hesitant to place orders amidst the ongoing political deadlock regarding the country's fiscal plans.

The HCOB flash composite output index fell to 48.6 in January from 50.0 in December. Economists had forecast the index to rise to 50.1. The index has remained below the no-change threshold for the first time since October last year.

The services PMI dropped unexpectedly to a nine-month low of 47.9 in January from 50.1 in the previous month. The score was seen at 50.3. The factory PMI rose to 51.0 from 50.7 in the prior month. The index was forecast to fall to 50.4 in January.

A report from INSEE showed France's manufacturing business climate indicator rose to 105 in January 2026 from 102 in December 2025, above forecasts of 101 and remaining above its long-term average of 100 for a second month. This marked the highest reading since July 2022.

data.title: Why power makes leaders see threats everywhere: Klement
data.description:
By Joachim Klement

From Moscow to Washington, big power politics have returned to the global stage. It would be easy to dismiss the actions of the last few years as large nations doing what they have always done, but recent insights in the field of behavioural geopolitics point to a different dynamic.

Increased power, perhaps counterintuitively, appears to breed increased fear of weaker competitors. This can trigger preventive action such as foreign interventions that, to outsiders, may look illogical.

This new geopolitical era was arguably kicked off in 2022 with Russia's full-scale invasion of its much smaller neighbour, Ukraine. But the shift back toward "big power" thinking truly went into hyperdrive with President Donald Trump's return to the White House.

First came the global trade war, with the U.S. president making it clear that he expected to set the terms of any trade negotiation, and then came the release of the administration’s National Security Strategy that resurrected the concept of "spheres of influence”.

But the real turning point came when the calendar turned to 2026. In less than one month, the Trump administration has conducted a military raid on Venezuela to seize the country's president, threatened to intervene in Iran and pursued a bellicose campaign to acquire Greenland (which at least for now, looks like it is de-escalating).

One way to make sense of what we're seeing is to look at geopolitics through the lens of behavioural psychology. In two papers from 2023 and 2025, Caleb Pomeroy from the University of Toronto used research on the impact of power on business leaders' thinking and actions to analyse international policymaking.

This research identified a broad range of behavioural patterns, from the benign to the consequential.

For example, people who feel powerful tend to make the opening argument in a debate or a negotiation, which typically gives them an advantage. But the research found that those who feel powerful also rely more heavily on stereotypes and discount others' views, which can lead them to ignore important input when making decisions.

That last feature can become especially troubling as people move up corporate and government hierarchies, where the issues they face become harder to resolve. As President Dwight D. Eisenhowerreportedly said, “No easy problems ever come to the President of the United States. If they are easy to solve, somebody else has solved them”.

As a result of the increasing complexity of the problems at hand and the limited availability of data on how to resolve them, business executives typically rely more on what Nobel-winning psychologist Daniel Kahneman called "System 1 thinking": fast, intuitive decision-making based on heuristics, emotions and experience rather than deliberate thought and deep analysis.

One major downside of System 1 thinking is that it often increases the fear of being challenged. Criticism naturally triggers our innate fight or flight instincts. And in a work setting, distancing oneself from the challenger is often not possible, creating a perception of being attacked without the possibility to deflect the criticism.

SEEING GHOSTS EVERYWHERE

Pomeroy’s research argues that these findings from the business world can translate to foreign relations. Using surveys and government documents, he demonstrates that, in many cases, the more dominant a politician thinks their country is, the more they typically fear other nations, big and small.

For example, in a 2020 survey among Russian policy elites, politicians who perceived Russia to be more powerful than the average respondent felt more threatened not only by the U.S. and the potential eastward expansion of NATO, but also by neighbouring Ukraine. No other psychological or demographic factor increased the perception of Ukraine as a threat to Russians.

Pomeroy also analysed U.S. diplomatic cables from the Cold War era. He showed that U.S. politicians and diplomats who expressed a stronger sense of U.S. global power perceived the Soviet Union, China and the Viet Cong (during the Vietnam War) to be bigger threats than those who were more circumspect about American might.

Indeed, an increased perception of power can turn even dovish people into foreign policy hawks. This may help explain ill-fated military interventions such as the U.S. invasion of Iraq in 2003 or the Soviet invasion of Afghanistan in 1979.

Of course, feeling threatened by a less powerful neighbour does not mean that the more dominant country will act on that perception, particularly if the nation's institutions inhibit rash actions. Moreover, there are plenty of examples throughout history of countries that have attacked other nations when they felt their grip on power slipping.

But Pomeroy's research highlights one unexpected way that power may distort the decision-making process of policy elites – something investors should keep in mind when trying to navigate this new, rapidly evolving geopolitical landscape.

(The views expressed here are those of Joachim Klement, an investment strategist for Panmure Liberum.)

data.title: OneIM Acquisition Corp Completes Initial Public Offering
data.description:
On January 15, 2026, OneIM Acquisition Corp successfully completed its IPO, raising $287.5 million.

Key Highlights:

  • IPO included 28,750,000 units, priced at $10.00 each.
  • Simultaneous private sale generated an additional $2 million.
  • Proceeds placed in a U.S.-based trust account for future use.
  • Company aims to complete a business combination by January 2028.
  • Audited balance sheet issued, reflecting IPO proceeds.

Original SEC Filing: OneIM Acquisition Corp. [ OIMAU ] - 8-K - Jan. 22, 2026

data.title: LanzaTech Global Signs Multiple Material Agreements
data.description:
LanzaTech Global announced a series of financing-related agreements to strengthen its capital structure and liquidity. The company raised $20 million through private placement Subscription Agreements, issuing 4,000,000 common shares at $5.00 per share plus 510,968 bonus shares, with one investor receiving board observer rights. A Second Amended and Restated Certificate of Designation triggered the mandatory conversion of all Series A Preferred into 3,250,322 common shares, removing mandatory redemption provisions. Concurrently, LanzaTech issued and amended a Warrant to the preferred holder, now exercisable through December 31, 2026 with automatic cashless exercise before expiry, and entered a Waiver Agreement extending the registration filing timeline for warrant shares.

Agreement 1: LanzaTech Global Signs Subscription Agreements With Institutional Investors

  • Agreement type: Private placement subscription agreements for common stock (PIPE)
  • Counterparty: Institutional Investors
  • Signed / Effective: Jan 21 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: At will
  • Reason: Raise capital and enhance liquidity

Agreement 2: LanzaTech Global Signs Second Amended and Restated Certificate of Designation With LanzaTech Global SPV

  • Agreement type: Second Amended and Restated Certificate of Designation for Series A Preferred Stock
  • Counterparty: LanzaTech Global SPV
  • Signed / Effective: Jan 21 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: At will
  • Reason: Simplify capital structure via mandatory preferred conversion

Agreement 3: LanzaTech Global Signs Warrant Agreement Amendment With LanzaTech Global SPV

  • Agreement type: Warrant to purchase 7,800,000 common shares with amended exercise terms
  • Counterparty: LanzaTech Global SPV
  • Signed / Effective: Jan 21 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: Until Dec 31 2026
  • Reason: Provide flexibility on timing of warrant exercise

Agreement 4: LanzaTech Global Signs Waiver Agreement With LanzaTech Global SPV

  • Agreement type: Waiver of registration rights timing with revised filing deadline
  • Counterparty: LanzaTech Global SPV
  • Signed / Effective: Jan 21 2026 / same
  • Duration / Termination: At will
  • Reason: Extend timeline for resale registration

Original SEC Filing: LanzaTech Global, Inc. [ LNZA ] - 8-K - Jan. 22, 2026

data.title: 1st Source Corporation Reports Record Annual Earnings
data.description:
1st Source Corporation, the parent company of 1st Source Bank, has released its latest SEC 8-K filing, revealing record financial performance for the year 2025. The company, which serves the northern half of Indiana and southwest Michigan, has demonstrated significant growth across various financial metrics, continuing its trend of strong performance in the financial sector.

Financial Highlights

For the year 2025, 1st Source Corporation reported a record net income of $158.28 million, marking a 19.34% increase from the $132.62 million earned in 2024. The diluted net income per common share also saw a substantial rise to $6.41, up 19.59% from $5.36 in the previous year. However, the fourth quarter net income of $41.14 million was down 2.73% from the previous quarter but up 30.87% from the same quarter in 2024.

The company's return on average assets increased to 1.76% for the full year 2025, compared to 1.52% in 2024. Similarly, the return on average common shareholders’ equity rose to 13.16% from 12.54% in the previous year. The tax-equivalent net interest margin for 2025 was 4.07%, up 43 basis points from 2024.

Business and Operational Highlights

1st Source Corporation experienced growth in both loans and deposits. Average loans and leases grew by $336.29 million, a 5.10% increase from 2024, reaching $6.93 billion. Average deposits increased by $263.33 million, up 3.70% to $7.38 billion. The company also launched a new platform called Online & Mobile Banking for Business, aimed at providing small business owners with a fast, reliable, and convenient way to manage their business accounts online.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

The Board of Directors approved a cash dividend of $0.40 per common share, an 11.11% increase from the previous year's dividend. Additionally, the company repurchased 230,036 shares for treasury, reducing common shareholders’ equity by $13.87 million. The company also received several accolades, including being named among Piper Sandler’s Sm-All Stars for the third consecutive year and receiving a “Superior” Bauer 5-Star Rating.

Management's Perspective

Andrea G. Short, President and Chief Executive Officer, expressed satisfaction with the company's performance, highlighting the record net income and the 38th consecutive year of dividend growth. She attributed the positive results to higher rates on investment securities, stable rates on loans and leases, and lower deposit and short-term borrowing rates. Executive Chairman Christopher J. Murphy III also noted the company's inclusion in Piper Sandler’s Sm-All Stars and the receipt of the Bauer 5-Star Rating as significant achievements.

Future Outlook

While the report did not provide specific forward-looking statements, the company's strong financial performance and strategic initiatives suggest a positive outlook for the future. The continued focus on expanding digital services and maintaining a conservative capital structure positions 1st Source Corporation well for sustained growth.

Original SEC Filing: 1ST SOURCE CORP [ SRCE ] - 8-K - Jan. 22, 2026

data.title: The 10-Point: The Wall Street Journal's Guide to the Day's Top News
data.description:
By Emma Tucker

In a dizzying three weeks on the world stage, President Trump deposed Venezuela's leader, threatened its neighbors with military action, raised the specter of new strikes on Iran and ignited — then tamped down — a diplomatic crisis with Europe over his efforts to acquire Greenland. At each fresh turn, Congress has found itself scrambling to keep up, sparking new debates among lawmakers about how to claw back constitutional powers from the presidency over matters of foreign policy in the Trump era.

Today's Headlines

A resolution to rein in Trump's powers to wage war in Venezuela was narrowly defeated in the House, after Republican leaders hustled a lawmaker back from Texas to cast the deciding vote. It failed in a tie vote of 215-215.

Elon Musk is opening his wallet and reigniting his political apparatus for the 2026 midterm elections.

China has seized upon the disarray left by Trump within the trans-Atlantic alliance, trying to entice U.S. allies with the promise of reliable trade partnerships.

Vice President JD Vance placed himself at the center of a clash between the federal government and state and local officials over immigration enforcement, calling for greater cooperation with ICE.

Venezuela's interim government unveiled a bill to loosen the state's iron grip on its beleaguered oil industry, a move aimed at attracting U.S. energy companies.

Live From The Markets

TikTok officially established a joint venture that would allow it to keep operating in the U.S., the company said, resolving a yearslong fight to address Washington's national-security concerns.

Trump's push for a credit-card interest-rate cap could be a problem for issuers of plastic. But if the experience of other industries is any guide, investors might want to consider buying bank stocks, writes Telis Demos.

Read It Here First

The West stepped back from the brink. But Europe's distrust of America lingers.

The crisis over Greenland, though now defused, already has many European leaders worrying about long-term damage. Trump's approach has also prompted leaders in Europe and Canada to focus more on reducing their countries' economic, technological and military dependence on the U.S., the kind of derisking previously reserved for China and Russia. Some European officials have started seriously worrying about the exposure of their economies to U.S. software, payment systems and communications platforms that could be switched off or disrupted in case of an escalating conflict, reports Yaroslav Trofimov.

Congress can't claw back war powers from Trump.

Many U.S. lawmakers — Democrats and a small but growing number of Republicans — are searching for ways to rein in the president. At stake, they say, is the U.S.-led global order that's been in place since the end of World War II. So far, these lawmakers concede they are failing. The latest Republican-controlled Congress has accelerated a decadeslong shift giving the White House greater authority to take on America's adversaries and even some allies, write Robbie Gramer and Lindsay Wise.

Expert Take

Q: How can a 'raise' from Social Security result in less take-home pay for retirees?

Nearly six million seniors owe extra Medicare premiums called Irmaa, and these charges are set to rise, writes Laura Saunders.

A: The short answer is that rising Medicare premiums ate into their monthly benefit.

Now here's more: For 2026, Social Security's inflation adjustment is 2.8%. Despite this "raise," some seniors will see their monthly benefits shrink.

That's because Medicare premiums are based on the overall cost of the Medicare program, and its costs and premiums have been rising. These premiums are subtracted from the monthly Social Security payments, so they can reduce a senior's "take-home" pay.

Nearly six million higher-earning seniors face yet another Medicare charge that affects their Social Security payment. This charge is based on income and is called Irmaa, for "Income related monthly adjustment amount."

Congress enacted Irmaa to have higher earners pick up a greater portion of their own Medicare costs. Annual Irmaa charges in 2026 for Part B Medicare (for doctors and outpatient care) range from about $974 to about $5,844 per recipient, depending on income.

According to the 2025 Medicare Trustees Report, overall Part B Irmaa charges are expected to increase 30% from 2026 to 2030-so Irmaa's bite will likely grow as well.

See The Story

We have no idea how to code. So we got Claude to code this article for us.

Using Anthropic's Claude Code, two WSJ columnists with no formal programming background vibe coded this article — chat bubbles, Easter eggs and all. Vibe-coding apps aren't new, but Claude Code has proved to be a leap ahead in capabilities and smarts. The results are wondrous and unsettling: People without a lick of coding experience are building things that once required trained software developers.

Happening Today

U.S., Russian and Ukrainian negotiators are holding trilateral talks in Abu Dhabi.

Economic data: University of Michigan consumer sentiment for January

Earnings: Comerica, SLB

Helen Macdonald's critically acclaimed memoir, "H Is for Hawk," lands on the big screen, starring Claire Foy. Check out everything else on this week's arts calendar.

Number Of The Day:

63%

The amount natural-gas prices have jumped this week in response to forecasts pointing to some of the coldest, snowiest weather in years. The forecasts have stoked fears of a repeat of the deadly winter storm that froze Texas in 2021 and left millions of people without electricity for days. Energy producers and utilities are preparing for the worst. Subzero temperatures are in store for Minneapolis, Chicago and Detroit starting today. New York and Washington, D.C., are expected to be buried in snow by the end of the weekend.

And Finally...

Dana White's empire of testosterone now stretches to the White House.

When asked what connects his two spheres of influence — mixed martial arts and Trump's MAGA base — Ultimate Fighting Championship mogul Dana White has a simple answer: "Testosterone." White has built an empire off it. Over more than two decades, he's taken the UFC from obscurity to an operation estimated to be worth more than $20 billion by parent TKO Group Holdings. White occupies a unique position in the zeitgeist: A sports kingpin, he's also part of a circle of Trump whisperers and influencers.
